title: Hormuz Ceasefire Collapses, Russia Bans Diesel Exports, and a 500% Tariff Bill Looms Over Russian Energy Buyers
subhead: The US-Iran ceasefire disintegrated after tanker attacks in Hormuz, Russia imposed a full diesel export ban deepening a global fuel crunch, and bipartisan senators reached a deal on legislation threatening 500% tariffs on Russian energy buyers — all while the IEA projects the first annual drop in global oil demand since 2020.

sections.heading: Hormuz Ceasefire Unravels as Iran Presses Control of Shipping Lanes
sections.body:
President Trump declared the US-Iran ceasefire 'OVER' on July 10, after Iran attacked three commercial tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz via a US-protected southern corridor along Oman's coast. The US responded with strikes on Iranian military targets, and Iran's armed forces subsequently attacked US military infrastructure in Bahrain and Kuwait, according to Iranian claims reported by News18 and others.

The core dispute is over who controls Hormuz. Iran insists that all vessel traffic must pass through its northern territorial waters and that administration of the strait 'rests exclusively with Iran,' as Tehran's UN diplomat stated on July 10, according to AP. The US, by contrast, has been escorting 5-8 million bpd through the Omani southern corridor and demands that Iran publicly declare the strait open and commit to halting attacks on shipping.

The June memorandum of understanding between Washington and Tehran never defined the specific shipping routes — it only said Iran would 'make arrangements using its best efforts' to ensure safe passage. According to David Goldwyn, former State Department special envoy for international energy affairs, the MOU 'essentially punted that issue.' Maritime intelligence firm Windward described the escalation as 'the most significant since the opening phase' of the war. US officials told reporters that a hardline faction in Tehran carried out the tanker strikes, and that Iranian leaders privately acknowledged the attacks were a mistake — but Trump responded with force regardless, making clear there would be consequences irrespective of which faction was responsible, AP reported.

Traffic through the strait has fallen sharply since July 8, especially through the Omani route, according to Windward analysts cited by Malay Mail. Iran's Revolutionary Guard warned that US military 'interference in determining the routes of maritime traffic' would disrupt the reopening process and put countries' interests at risk, Press TV reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire's collapse is not a one-off event but a structural pattern: each temporary pause collapses because the fundamental disagreement over Hormuz control remains unresolved. Iran's leverage depends on keeping the strait contested; the US cannot accept Iranian veto power over international shipping. Every cycle of escalation raises insurance premiums, diverts tanker traffic, and tightens global fuel supply.

sections.heading: Russia Bans Diesel Exports as Ukrainian Strikes Cripple Refineries
sections.body:
Russia imposed a full ban on diesel exports on July 8, effective through July 31, Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak announced. Unlike previous partial restrictions, the ban applies to both producers and traders. The move came as Russia faces its deepest domestic fuel crisis in years: long queues have formed at gas stations across the country, and rationing has been implemented in over 20 regions, Oil & Gas Journal reported.

The crisis was triggered by Ukrainian drone strikes on major Russian refineries. The Moscow refinery, which previously supplied about 40% of fuel for the Moscow region, suffered particularly severe damage, according to OGJ. Ukraine's general staff confirmed on July 10 that it struck refineries, an oil terminal and depot across Russia, as well as tankers in the Sea of Azov — part of a new 'long-range impact' command Kyiv established to intensify strikes on Russian energy infrastructure, Reuters reported.

Russian diesel and gasoil exports had already been falling for months before the ban. Seaborne loadings fell to just 234,000 bpd in the first 10 days of July, down from 400,000 bpd in June and the 2025 average of roughly 817,000 bpd, according to Kpler data cited by SCMP. Russia accounted for about 11% of global diesel supplies last year. The export ban has sent diesel prices surging in Europe and the US. US government data showed an inventory draw of over 4.5 million barrels of diesel in the week ending July 3, bringing stocks to 97.8 million barrels — 6% below the five-year average.

Novak said Russia would begin importing fuel this month to bridge the domestic supply gap, with gasoline shipments from India reportedly already underway.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's diesel ban is a symptom of a deeper shift: Ukrainian strikes are systematically degrading Russian refining capacity, and Moscow is prioritizing domestic stability over export revenue. The global diesel market, already tight from the Hormuz disruption, now faces the loss of the world's second-largest diesel exporter. The knock-on effects hit agriculture, transport, and power generation worldwide.

sections.heading: Senators Reach Deal on 500% Tariff Bill Targeting Russian Energy Buyers
sections.body:
Four US senators — Republicans Lindsey Graham and Roger Wicker, Democrats Richard Blumenthal and Jeanne Shaheen — announced on July 10 that they had reached an agreement with the Trump administration to advance the updated Sanctioning Russia Act. The bill, which has 84 Senate co-sponsors, threatens tariffs of at least 500% on goods from countries that continue purchasing Russian oil, natural gas, uranium, and petroleum products, according to CryptoBriefing and News18.

India has been specifically named by supporters of the legislation. Senator Graham wrote on X in June 2025: 'To China and India: if you continue to prop up Putin's war machine, you'll have nobody to blame but yourself.' India continued buying Russian crude under a US Treasury general licence issued during the US-Iran war, but that licence expired on June 17. The bill also includes a provision allowing the President to grant a 180-day waiver to specific countries if deemed in the US national security interest.

The final tariff provisions are reported to have been softened from the original draft, though specific details have not yet been made public. The bipartisan deal marks a significant escalation in the legislative push to squeeze Russia's energy revenue and puts India and China — Moscow's two largest energy customers — directly in the crosshairs of US economic pressure.
sections.whyItMatters: If enacted, this legislation would fundamentally alter the calculus for countries still buying Russian energy. India, which built its refining margins on discounted Russian crude, faces a direct threat to its economic model. The 180-day waiver provision gives the White House leverage to use the tariff threat as a bargaining chip rather than an automatic trigger — making this as much a diplomatic tool as an economic one.

sections.heading: IEA: First Annual Drop in Global Oil Demand Since 2020, China Cuts Imports by 6 Million Bpd
sections.body:
The International Energy Agency projects global oil demand will fall by about 1 million bpd in 2026 — the first annual decline since the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Global demand averaged just 97.9 million bpd in May, down 5.3 million bpd from a year earlier, the IEA reported, according to AP.

The drop was driven primarily by Asia, which relies heavily on Middle Eastern crude. China's demand fell by 1.5 million bpd, a 9% decline — the largest of any country. S&P Global's Jim Burkhard told AP that China deliberately cut purchases by roughly 6 million bpd from the global market as prices rose during the spring, drawing on its massive strategic stockpiles instead. 'What China said is, prices are high, there's a crisis. We have this huge inventory stock, we can sustain demand. We're just going to cut by 50% the amount of crude oil we buy,' Burkhard said.

The US was the notable exception: gasoline consumption increased in the second quarter of 2026 despite pump prices roughly 50% above prewar levels. The IEA also warned that renewed US-Iran escalation could threaten its projected 2027 oil market surplus.

Simultaneously, the IEA reported that UAE crude output hit a record 4.1 million bpd in June, nearly double the 2 million-plus bpd at the start of the Hormuz crisis. The UAE, which left OPEC effective May 1, has been exporting crude through the Fujairah bypass and in 'dark mode' through the strait, Oilprice.com reported citing IEA data. ADNOC is accelerating plans for a new pipeline to double Fujairah export capacity by 2027 and has announced $55 billion in upstream and downstream investment over the next two years.
sections.whyItMatters: The demand shock is real and massive: 5.3 million bpd wiped from global consumption in a single month. China's strategic drawdown is the biggest single factor — Beijing chose to run down reserves rather than pay wartime prices, effectively deflating the oil market's traditional price signal. Meanwhile, the UAE's record output shows how the Hormuz crisis is reshaping OPEC itself, with Abu Dhabi exiting the cartel to capture market share while it can.

sections.heading: Iranian Oil Piles Up at Sea as Chinese Teapots Switch to Cheaper Crudes
sections.body:
Iranian oil supplies stranded at sea are surging after Tehran ramped up exports during the brief ceasefire window, only to find buyers scarce. Reuters reported on July 10 that China's independent teapot refiners — the primary customers for Iranian crude — have turned to cheaper supplies from Iraq, the UAE, and Qatar instead.

The US reimposed sanctions on Iranian oil on July 7 after revoking the waiver that had been part of the ceasefire MOU. Iran's foreign ministry called the reimposition a 'material breach' of the agreement. The combination of renewed sanctions, renewed tanker attacks in Hormuz, and China's demand drawdown has left Iranian cargoes struggling to find buyers, even at deep discounts.

The surplus at sea is a sharp reversal from the brief window when the June MOU's sanctions relief had prompted Tehran to push more cargoes out. Now those cargoes are floating in limbo — another consequence of the ceasefire's ambiguous terms, which temporarily lifted sanctions on Iranian oil without establishing a durable framework for its sale and transport.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's floating storage glut shows how quickly the ceasefire's temporary sanctions relief became a trap. Tehran shipped crude assuming the window would hold; it didn't. The result is a self-inflicted supply overhang that further weakens Iran's bargaining position even as it tries to assert control over Hormuz.

sections.heading: India Builds Strategic Reserves and Signs Australia Uranium Deal
sections.body:
India's Oil and Natural Gas Corporation (ONGC) approved construction of a 1.75 million metric ton (roughly 13 million barrel) strategic petroleum reserve in Mangaluru, the company announced on July 9. The facility, designated a project of national importance, is part of a five-site SPR expansion plan covering Chandikhol, Bina, Bikaner, Mangaluru, and Padur.

India currently holds only about 9.5 days of import coverage in strategic reserves — far below the IEA's recommended 90-day minimum, and dwarfed by China's roughly 90 days and Japan's 200 days. The FY27 Budget allocation for SPR infrastructure was slashed to just ₹200 crore from ₹5,876 crore in FY26, according to Business Standard, making the ONGC-led approach a departure from the government's earlier underfunding.

In parallel, India and Australia signed a landmark uranium supply agreement on July 10 during PM Modi's visit to Melbourne. The deal operationalises a civil nuclear cooperation agreement that had been stalled for over a decade, enabled by India's SHANTI Act of December 2025, which opened the country's civil nuclear sector to private and foreign participation. Australia, which holds nearly 28% of the world's known uranium reserves, will become a key long-term nuclear fuel supplier. India aims to expand its nuclear capacity from the current 8.8 GW to 100 GW by 2047, according to Firstpost.
sections.whyItMatters: India is belatedly confronting a strategic vulnerability exposed by the Hormuz crisis: its near-zero reserve buffer. The SPR expansion and uranium deal both reflect a push for energy security autonomy that will reshape procurement patterns for years. The uranium deal also carries geopolitical weight — it deepens Quad energy integration and gives Australia a stake in India's nuclear buildout.

sections.heading: IAEA Loses Oversight of Iran's Nuclear Program After US-Israeli Strikes
sections.body:
The United Nations confirmed that the IAEA has lost oversight of Iran's nuclear program following US-Israeli strikes on nuclear sites earlier in the war. The disruption of 'continuity of knowledge' at nuclear sites means inspectors can no longer verify production and inventory, Anadolu Agency reported citing UN statements.

Senior US officials are increasingly pessimistic about the prospects of a nuclear agreement with Iran, the Wall Street Journal reported. The Trump administration has warned Tehran it must address the nuclear issue, but the destruction of monitoring infrastructure has made verification of any future deal far more difficult.

The loss of IAEA oversight is compounded by the internal power struggle in Tehran following the killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in earlier strikes and his burial on July 9. US officials told reporters that hardline factions within Iran's leadership are actively working to sabotage negotiations, making it harder to re-establish monitoring even if a political agreement is reached, AP reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The IAEA's inability to verify Iran's nuclear activities is a threshold moment. Without inspectors on the ground, any future deal would be built on unverifiable assumptions. The loss of continuity of knowledge means the international community is effectively flying blind on Iran's enrichment and stockpile levels — a situation that will make any negotiation far harder to conclude and verify.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire formally ended this week after tanker attacks in Hormuz exposed the core disagreement both sides had papered over: Iran claims exclusive control of the strait, the US insists on free navigation. Each cycle of escalation and pause leaves Hormuz more contested and global fuel supply tighter. Russia's diesel export ban — a direct consequence of Ukrainian drone warfare on its refineries — has compounded the supply shock, with diesel prices spiking worldwide. The bipartisan push for 500% tariffs on Russian energy buyers creates a new threat to the India-China-Russia crude trade corridor, while the IEA's first projected annual demand decline since 2020 confirms that the combination of high prices and Hormuz disruption is already destroying consumption. On the strategic reserves front, India is finally scrambling to build buffers and secure uranium from Australia, a sign that middle powers are hedging hard against a world where chokepoint risk and sanctions risk are converging.
sources: AP, CNBC, Reuters, SCMP, Oil & Gas Journal, Oilprice.com, Business Standard, Firstpost, Malay Mail, CryptoBriefing, News18, Financial Express, IEA, Windward, Press TV, Wall Street Journal, Kpler, S&P Global, Bloomberg, Anadolu Agency
